findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a well-defined, multiloculated, expansile lytic bone lesion located in the metadiaphysis. It demonstrates thin, intact sclerotic margins and prominent endosteal scalloping, resulting in a characteristic 'ballooning' or 'blow-out' appearance of the affected bone.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the presence of delicate, horizontally oriented internal bony trabeculations or septations. If MRI is available, multiple cystic spaces containing fluid-fluid levels of varying signal intensities would be seen, reflecting hemorrhage of different ages with sedimentation.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of cortical destruction, an aggressive soft tissue mass, or a disorganized periosteal reaction to suggest a malignant process like a telangiectatic osteosarcoma. Furthermore, there is no ground-glass matrix to suggest fibrous dysplasia.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the highly expansile, multiloculated nature of the lytic lesion with characteristic internal fluid-fluid levels indicative of hemorrhage.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Aneurysmal bone cyst, given the markedly expansile, multiloculated lytic appearance in a young patient. The presence of fluid-fluid levels on cross-sectional imaging, representing blood products of varying ages settling within cystic cavities, is a hallmark feature of this benign neoplasm.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Fibrous dysplasia, however this typically demonstrates a classic 'ground-glass' matrix and a thick sclerotic rind of reactive bone rather than a purely lytic, multiloculated appearance. Furthermore, while fibrous dysplasia can expand the bone, it tends to cause more protracted, elongated remodeling rather than the focal, balloon-like expansion seen here.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Unicameral bone cyst, given that these are also common, benign, lucent lesions in children that can present with a pathologic fracture, and misdiagnosing it could alter surgical management. However, simple bone cysts are typically centrally located, less expansile, lack the prominent fluid-fluid levels of hemorrhage seen in ABCs, and classically exhibit the 'fallen fragment' sign when fractured.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately assess the full extent of the lesion, confirm the presence of an intact cortical shell, and rule out any underlying primary bone tumor that might have caused a secondary aneurysmal bone cyst.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine the growth rate of the lesion and to identify if it arose from a preexisting benign bone lesion such as a giant cell tumor or chondroblastoma.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the affected region without and with intravenous contrast, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely demonstrating the characteristic fluid-fluid levels, confirming the presence of an intact periosteal membrane, and identifying any solid nodular components that might suggest a secondary ABC.
management.mriSequences: I would specify fluid-sensitive T2-weighted or STIR sequences to optimally demonstrate the characteristic fluid-fluid levels caused by blood product sedimentation, alongside T1 pre- and post-contrast sequences to assess for enhancing solid septations or an underlying primary tumor.
management.intervention: If a secondary underlying tumor is suspected on MRI, I would specify an image-guided core biopsy targeting any solid, enhancing peripheral tissue rather than the central fluid cavities.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Musculoskeletal MDT. My report would clearly document the exact dimensions of the expansile lesion, the integrity of the surrounding cortical bone, and any proximity to the adjacent growth plate or joint space to guide appropriate surgical curettage and bone grafting.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a well-defined, multiloculated, expansile lytic bone lesion located in the metadiaphysis. It demonstrates thin, intact sclerotic margins and prominent endosteal scalloping, resulting in a characteristic 'ballooning' or 'blow-out' appearance of the affected bone. Supporting features include the presence of delicate, horizontally oriented internal bony trabeculations or septations. If MRI is available, multiple cystic spaces containing fluid-fluid levels of varying signal intensities would be seen, reflecting hemorrhage of different ages with sedimentation. Importantly, there is no evidence of cortical destruction, an aggressive soft tissue mass, or a disorganized periosteal reaction to suggest a malignant process like a telangiectatic osteosarcoma. Furthermore, there is no ground-glass matrix to suggest fibrous dysplasia. The feature that most influences my thinking is the highly expansile, multiloculated nature of the lytic lesion with characteristic internal fluid-fluid levels indicative of hemorrhage.

The appearances are most in keeping with Aneurysmal bone cyst, given the markedly expansile, multiloculated lytic appearance in a young patient. The presence of fluid-fluid levels on cross-sectional imaging, representing blood products of varying ages settling within cystic cavities, is a hallmark feature of this benign neoplasm. The principle differential would be Fibrous dysplasia, however this typically demonstrates a classic 'ground-glass' matrix and a thick sclerotic rind of reactive bone rather than a purely lytic, multiloculated appearance. Furthermore, while fibrous dysplasia can expand the bone, it tends to cause more protracted, elongated remodeling rather than the focal, balloon-like expansion seen here. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Unicameral bone cyst, given that these are also common, benign, lucent lesions in children that can present with a pathologic fracture, and misdiagnosing it could alter surgical management. However, simple bone cysts are typically centrally located, less expansile, lack the prominent fluid-fluid levels of hemorrhage seen in ABCs, and classically exhibit the 'fallen fragment' sign when fractured.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately assess the full extent of the lesion, confirm the presence of an intact cortical shell, and rule out any underlying primary bone tumor that might have caused a secondary aneurysmal bone cyst. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine the growth rate of the lesion and to identify if it arose from a preexisting benign bone lesion such as a giant cell tumor or chondroblastoma. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the affected region without and with intravenous contrast, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely demonstrating the characteristic fluid-fluid levels, confirming the presence of an intact periosteal membrane, and identifying any solid nodular components that might suggest a secondary ABC. I would specify fluid-sensitive T2-weighted or STIR sequences to optimally demonstrate the characteristic fluid-fluid levels caused by blood product sedimentation, alongside T1 pre- and post-contrast sequences to assess for enhancing solid septations or an underlying primary tumor. If a secondary underlying tumor is suspected on MRI, I would specify an image-guided core biopsy targeting any solid, enhancing peripheral tissue rather than the central fluid cavities. This should go to the Musculoskeletal MDT. My report would clearly document the exact dimensions of the expansile lesion, the integrity of the surrounding cortical bone, and any proximity to the adjacent growth plate or joint space to guide appropriate surgical curettage and bone grafting.
